event: numbness and weakness in lower extremities
presumptive diagnosis of Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) or acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP)
past medical history included type 2 diabetes mellitus
had an episode of necrotizing pancreatitis due to gallstones
complicated by severe protein-calorie malnutrition requiring total parenteral nutrition (TPN)
discontinued TPN and returned to normal diet
albuminocytologic dissociation in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan initially showed small vessel ischemic changes
received five days of empiric intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) treatment
flaccid paralysis of all four extremities
Nutritional risk screening (NRS-2002) resulted in a score of 5 points
required an intravenous infusion of norepinephrine
provisional diagnosis was AIDP complicated by septic shock
given concerns regarding sepsis, meropenem, vancomycin, and anidulafungin treatment was initiated
empirical treatment with high-dose intravenous thiamine was commenced
electroencephalogram (EEG) did not show seizure activity
showed diffuse slow waves consistent with severe metabolic encephalopathy
brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan was performed
showed hyperintensity of the bilateral medial thalamus on T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) axial images
after four doses of high-dose thiamine therapy
norepinephrine and antimicrobial treatment were discontinued
mentation and weakness continued to gradually improve
transferred out of the intensive care unit (ICU) after 14 days
thiamine supplementation was continued throughout her hospital stay
